translation.content: It's 1996, and a young Fumiya Tanaka is shelling out hefty yet minimal percussive techno at Club Rockets in Osaka to an audience enraptured (listen out for the intermittent screams of joy when Tanaka mixes in a new track). Released as Mix-Up, the 90-minute recording captures Tanaka sounding rather like Jeff Mills or Surgeon, a steel-toed style he also explored, with a wink, on 1995's I Am Not A DJ. Fumiya Tanaka's creative arc has seen him move away from these thunderous sounds to warmer shades of house and minimal. Since 2016, he's found a home on the inimitable German minimal label, crafting out a distinctive sound within the labels roster with an affection for tumbling basslines and spooky atmospheres. From 1996 to 2023, Tanaka ran a party series in Tokyo, Osaka and Berlin called "Chaos," which encapsulates the ethos of freedom Tanaka brings to a party. "When you hear music you've never heard before and encounter unknown territory, you will be so happy and totally absorbed," he told us back in 2019. "I want to keep that feeling." RA.964 achieves exactly that. Nearly three decades removed from his burst onto the scene, and after a good few years of asking, Tanaka's RA Podcast captures the Perlon maestro in full house mode. Recorded at a Slapfunk party in Manchester, Tanaka keeps the vibe funked-up, chunky and warm, punctuated by the occasional big breakdown and the odd lick of garage rudeness. No tracklist for now—but as Tanaka knows well, half the fun lies in the mystery. – Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? I'm living with my child and building my musical life. It is hard work at times, but it's really rewarding. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? It was recorded on the 4th of May 2024, at a Slapfunk party in Manchester. My gigs in Europe were in the UK, Germany and France in 1995. I have a lot of my musical influence from the UK, so it is always special for me when I play in the UK, as well as Berlin. The Slapfunk crew is really special. Doudou MD was the first to invite me to play at their party in Amsterdam. I'm thankful for her, and I'm always impressed by the crowd and the crew. It's always a special experience for me when I join them. My idea was to showcase my musical roots, while adapting to the Manchester crowd. An artist's ideas should never be grand—it's all about how creative you can be, taking a small idea, and stretching it. The DJ must adapt their selection for the crowd's vibe. I also carefully think about where the party is. The general theme I stick to is a completely natural approach. What are you looking forward to in the near future? It has been 16 months of not making music since my landlord kicked me out of my old studio. I'm spending my time doing other things now, but winter is here, so it's the right time to make music again. At the same time, I thought, if I stop making music, what can I do? I've experienced the possibilities when an artist only DJs for 16 months. What I've realized is that focusing on higher quality in DJing allows me to explore other creative ideas and manage my time better. I even have a production concept in mind, though I sometimes struggle with overthinking and a dilemma that dominates my passion. That's why I’m really looking forward to my new studio being completed. Music can rescue a human being. 2025 will mark 30 years since your debut record, Four Tracks, with anniversaries for the Unknown Possibility series not far behind. What are your fondest memories of that period in your life? It's been 14 years since I moved to Berlin—everything has gone so fast. When I was younger, I believed that I could change the world through music. I really believed it. Being young gave me both the advantage of idealism and the privilege of pursuing what I wanted with passion. I did what I could and followed my dreams. Along the way, I made many mistakes but I've also gained a lot in the end. Back then, electronic music was just emerging in Japan. Some Detroit artists visited Japan for the first time, and Jeff Mills left a huge impact on the crowds. One of my most vivid memories is when I met Takkyu Ishino. We spent a lot of time in my small flat, talking seriously about the future, mixed with plenty of humor and jokes throughout the night. At the same time, I had just started making music in that small flat, using really cheap equipment and relying on ideas I shared with my friends in Osaka. We did so many crazy things together. It's hard to explain exactly what we did—it was a ridiculous yet amazing time. A guy from Sony Records once visited my small flat to scout me for a major debut. I misunderstood the situation at the time, but I think I would still be doing the same things as now—DJing and making music. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? My party, CHAOS, had a big impact, as well as Get Perlonized. Both were great experiences, helping me fulfil my potential while I was still learning and developing. On my European tours in the '90s, I used to spend my free weekends dancing at warehouse parties in the UK. Also Club Rockets in Osaka—it's one of the most important clubs for me. I used to organise parties there, and despite many conflicts between conservative people, we were free to do what we wanted to do. Together, we helped establish an electronic music scene in Osaka, based on what we had experienced elsewhere. Finally: You had a run of mix classics in the '90s, chiefly Mix-Up Vol.4 and I Am Not A DJ. What are your thoughts about the value of a good DJ mix today? What is this? It's the mysterious groove that only a human DJ can create—the unique selection of tracks, the distinctive timing of mixes, the subtle EQ control, and the happy accidents. These elements are the remaining possibilities that set good DJs apart. At the same time, with technological advancements, it seems likely that mixes that sound flawless, familiar and perfectly crafted could soon be artificially generated. If such mixes dominate the market and people grow accustomed to them, a new perception of value might emerge where these artificial mixes are considered sufficient. The human element represents the true value of a good DJ. The best DJs maintain their own groove, reading the vibe of the crowd and selecting tracks that resonate with the moment. Ultimately, the value of a DJ will depend on the kind of experience the crowd seeks.
translation.blurb: Big basslines and big grooves from the longtime Perlon affiliate.
